There's probably no one ever that didn't want to get promoted."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"It's a common topic in people's career, but a lot of times people don't understand why they're not getting promoted, they're not sure people are looking for to get promoted. You've promoted a lot of people and you've gone through a lot of promotions."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"What would be your advice to give people who are trying to get promoted and just haven't been promoted? What would you suggest people in that position generally do?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Yeah, it's a great question. I think that we want to kind of understand why, and oftentimes asking your manager won't reveal the answer. So then you'll start with that. I think that the answer of what you do is correlated with what's the real reason."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And I think that there may be, I'll suggest four kind of common things I've seen that really hold people back. And then depending on your environment, you have to decide how many of these apply. So I think the number one is that you just don't have advocacy."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"You need someone to see the magic in you to be promoted. There is many of your listeners who have that magic but maybe have a manager or a promotion team, it doesn't always have to be the manager itself, who doesn't see said magic."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And in that case, if you have the magic, you're in a bad setting and you just need to change. That could be a shift within the project. You could find a manager who sees it. It could be leaving the company."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I think the second that's very common now, Lenny, and I think it's coming up a ton, is the next role doesn't exist."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Mm-hmm."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"So this is not as present in hypergrowth because the next role always did exist. There was always growth, there was always hiring, you're always hiring people above you, below you, et cetera. Now, I think there's lots of examples of people who are really qualified and working at the next level, but the job doesn't exist."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"So you can't really create that job and ask them to be working at that next job if their position is mostly the previous one. Again, I feel like it's not that satisfying because it means you're still being held back, but it's radically different than if you're unqualified."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"These two are sort of more, the system is not in a position to advocate. The third is when you are being impatient. And I think the hardest ones that I think I've worked with is, the highest performers have succeeded because they have set their goals to be more aggressive than what was essentially average achievable."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"By default, we expect you to be two years in this role. They're like, \"Great, I'll see you in a year.\" And then they get frustrated when they can't do that, and leadership takes longer to absorb. It's more soft skills, it's more subtle."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Oftentimes it's based on impact, which is a lot of times lagging, and that tends to be frustrating. So if listeners are like, \"I know I'm used to being promoted annually and now I'm a leader and I'm not moving as quickly, it's time for me to go,\" I'm like, well, maybe that's working as intended."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"So impatience is a number three. And then the fourth one I think is about 50% of the cases where it's really, there is a development area but it isn't quite connected to the individual. The listener has a development area, it's substantive."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The manager is poor at identifying it, perhaps even doesn't see it, but the promotion committee does, the individual refuses to hear it, which is a very common one. Or they hear it and they just don't want to change it. And they don't do it because they're arrogant, they do it because it's like, \"This is who I am."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"You want me to be X and I'm Y, and that's what a Y is and I don't want to be X.\" This is the hardest one because this is where coaching and development and self-awareness come in."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Amazing. This super resonates. So just to summarize the four reasons you may not be getting promoted: One is, there's no advocate that sees your magic and understands that you're awesome. Two is, there's no actual role that's available and so there's nothing to get promoted to. And that's so true right now, there's just not."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Everyone's laying people off, they're getting rid of manager layers. I totally see that all over the place. Three is, you're probably just not being patient. Four, you actually have some work to do and you shouldn't be promoted."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Maybe to follow a thread on that first one, if someone doesn't see your magic, I see a lot of people just complaining that like, \"Oh, I'm doing so well, I'm so great and nobody understands it. No one gives me credit. No one really appreciates me.\""}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I don't know if there is an answer to this, but is there a way to help people see that no, you're actually not doing great versus you are and people just don't see it? What's a sign maybe? Maybe you're not as great as you think you might be."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The cheap answer is you have to get real feedback, not formal feedback. I think that the more scaled the company is, the more they have these systems in place which provide formal feedback."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"But honestly, we've run experiments where we said let's ignore the formal feedback, let's have a real conversation with my peers on how our teams are doing. The signal that comes out are dramatically different than the formal feedback."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"So what you're looking for when you feel like you're in this situation where you're not being seen, and it might be because there's a real issue, what you really want to dial into is, \"Let me get the ground truth as to what people are thinking,\" and you have to have very strong listener skills where we all have been in the discussion, where you're giving feedback to someone and the next thing that they tell you is they justify how you're wrong, that you missed this."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"\"Let me tell you about exactly why that situation that you're using wasn't...\" You have to be great at pulling feedback, listening to it. You have to triangulate it from people that don't see you all the time, that do see you all the time, your peers."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"But you have to create an environment of safety where people feel like there is no worry about retaliation or concern, et cetera."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And the more comfortable people are about giving feedback to you and the more you have the skills to pull it and you don't trust formal or you don't trust manager, the better shot you have of truly understanding what that real issue is and solving it."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"This reminds me of Jules Walter who's on the podcast. He gave a bunch of advice. I don't know if you saw that. I've had to accept feedback and get people to give you feedback. And one of its pieces of advice is, ask people for real feedback."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And no matter how much you're melting inside hearing it, just be like, \"Thank you so much for that.\" Because then, people feel like, \"All right, he's listening.\""}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I think Jules is a great, probably one of the world's best people in pulling feedback in my experience. I think that the one that even ones up it is, when I talk to Jules, Jules will look for feedback, then he'll repeat it back to me better than even I presented it."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And then I'd say, \"Well, let me now feel safer to even provide.\" Because anyone who's explaining it to a place that they all not only internalize it but they can articulate clearly understands and values it."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And that's the really powerful way is, \"So what you're saying is I just interrupt far too often and some ways it's almost to a point that it's annoying. Is that a fair assessment?\" \"Oh, that's actually not the words I use,\" but that's what really gets people comfortable in sharing with you what's really going on."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Amazing. I think we're discovering some of these people that have worked with you that have been on the podcast slowly."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Maybe while we're on this topic, I didn't expect to go here, but in terms of other tips for getting good feedback, is there anything else that just comes top of mind of how to get better feedback from people? Because it's hard to do. Most people talk about getting feedback and then don't, or they just don't know how."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"So one is just, you said repeat back exactly what they told you and be like very appreciative. Is there anything else?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I'd share out feedback. It's a little easier when you are a manager, but for example, most managers that are listening have a staff discussion. Maybe it's sort of awkward, but maybe you have a standup and you are giving notes to people. So as a manager, someone will come to me and they'll give me a piece of feedback."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The next Monday when I have my staff meeting, I'll make a comment about something and I'll say, \"Well, lot of this came because I got this great piece of feedback from...\" and I'll name the person, and I'm like, \"It really helped me see this challenge.\""}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Now, that feedback could be about me or about this project or about the team. And it might be positive, it might be constructive. People hear that and they're like, \"Wow, I get recognized for giving this guy feedback. Sign me up.\" You're always trying to find way to break down that barrier."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I love that tip. You talked about managers and how often managers are not great at managers. Maybe they don't identify development areas, maybe they're bad in other areas. So maybe just a question here of just, why are managers often not great?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And then two, if you're a new manager, I think a lot of listeners are maybe transitioning to management or about to transition, what's your advice for being successful as a new manager?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I'll start by saying that, in a hundred years when the archeologists look back and they see tech in the sort of early years of tech, the first 34 years, they'll say that the biggest surprise was how much we thought it was okay to not train managers."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The military probably didn't make that mistake for very long before they corrected it. And most immature industries really train managers, but boy, if you're a good coder, you are ready to manage. That's the way the industry works. If you can talk, you are ready to product manage."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"If you can product manage and ship something out the door, you should definitely tell people what to do. I think that there's such a loose coupling between the skills to be successful at building things and teaching people how to build."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"It's the difference between if you can make a good car, you must know how to make the factory that makes the car. I don't think that's true at all."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I think that this is a massive epidemic, that I think there's the thousand challenges that stem from this, whether it's challenges around bias, challenges around enabling coaching and teaching and solving development areas. My hope is that one day as an industry we find ways to improve and fix it. But podcasts like yours are actually quite meaningful steps."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I would say that your podcast might be more meaningful than most L&D departments in most organizations today. So that's powerful because you're having a tremendous amount of impact, and I think learning is essentially a lifelong opportunity and I think that is the type of resources that just didn't exist a decade ago."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I think to answer your question around what are the common pitfalls, if you're a first time manager listening or maybe someone who's considering it, I think there are probably two quick things that I would say you have to get bravely thoughtful about as you enter into this journey."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"One is, your challenge is going to be to share the steering wheel with the person or the set of people you are managing. And I think that there's this three modes that people have in their head. They're like, \"Oh, management is divide and conquering. You go there, I go there, we meet up.\""}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Or they'll say it's like riding a bike, or teaching to ride a bike, I should say. Someone starts out on the bicycle, I hold your hand, I let go, and then I hope that you fly."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I think it's more like the sidecar on the motorcycle, where person's driving the motorcycle and I'm on the sidecar and whether I like it or not, I'm attached, but I have this relatively specific role of giving counsel."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I think that that model of how do you share the steering wheel, not just say you got it or I got it or I got it for a while, and then I hand it to you is the key question."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And then I think that the second miss that people tend to have is they tend to, because they have power, by the way, organizational power, not because they've earned that power, they start managing whatever they define that to be. And what I find is that you're more like the vampire knocking on the door of someone."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"You have to be invited in. You just can't walk through the threshold. And I think that no matter how senior the person that is the manager, you have to earn the right to be the person's manager."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"So maybe to be specific, well, if I start managing someone, the thing I'd like to understand is like, \"Hey, well, what can I help you with?\" And they can invite me in. Oftentimes, the answer is, \"I don't need you. I actually wasn't excited about you as a manager. I don't need another layer between you and the CEO."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Get out.\" And I'm like, \"That's cool,\" because anything I say after that is just going to be annoying and it's going to backfire. Now, one day they will need help, and I will be in the sidecar waiting to say, \"Perhaps I can assist.\""}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And then when you finally get to that moment where you're invited in, you pick an area or two, and then you really partner with that person on that area."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I can give examples on that, but I generally think that it's this invitation picking specific and then making sure we're sharing the responsibility is the key set of notes that I would share with you."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"What's your take on the IC path, senior IC path, something that a lot of companies talk about? I know Meta is big on this right now, the layering managers and things like that. I find a lot of times there's a lot of talk about it and there's not really a real career opportunity there."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I guess, what's your just take on as that is a real option for most people trying to basically avoid the manager out and staying in IC, PM long term?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Yeah, I think that it's a little bit more acute now because of the backlash that we talked about between growth where management was perceived. So in this case, management was perceived as a way to drive expansion."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"So if you're in charge of expansion, you're managing the people that are doing the build and now we're doing a lot fewer things. So I think that's what's mandated this sort of growth in the IC track, for lack of a better term."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I think it is one of the best things that happened to our industry because what's happened is, in the last 10 years, and you can tell I'm particularly hard on our managers here, they've basically been promising ICs that early promoted into management. They didn't get taught, and now they're sort of average managers and promising ICs."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"But now the story that they tell and what they've built is not awesome."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"If I'm looking to hire, if I'm in a growth company and I'm the next hottest thing and I'm looking to hire someone and someone walks into the interview and said, \"Look, I've managed two people before and then I was in the charge of this thing, but they really did the details."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And then by the way before that, I was early in trying to get this thing out the door and then they picked me to be manager,\" I'm like, \"Okay, that's an interesting set of experiences. I don't have any other goals.\" And it was pretty sad. Exactly how you're describing."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"It was just like, \"I guess I'll just work here and I don't know, maybe I'll start another company, but I already did the thing I wanted to do.\""}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Your story is I think very inspiring because what you did is you said, \"I think the thing that I want to do is give, but I want to do it in my own way and I want to create something, but I want to do something that I think I can do for 30 years and I want to do it.\""}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"It has lots and lots of spokes to it. So you reinvented yourself professionally, but you created a new North Star."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"My sense is, for every one of you, Lenny, there's a hundred that could do that, that could do giving, that could do things that could scale, but that end up falling into what got them to be successful in act two and they get stuck."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"So this is the reason why when you hit your skip, keep looking for the next skip, is the point I'm trying to make."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And I think you're an inspiration for a lot of folks who have seen you transition and realizes life after just being a tech professional entrepreneur, there's got to be ways to do more of this for all of your listeners. And I think it's never too early to start thinking through."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"It's actually quite powering to think that you have such a long career. You can make mistakes and you can do some amazing things down the road."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Yeah. This is my fourth career, is what I realized. I was a engineer, then a founder, then a product manager, and now whatever this is."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Whatever this is."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Whatever this is. I guess, just to give people something inspiring, productive, what are maybe some examples of North Stars you've seen that people can evolve into? I guess one path is this path of content creation, helping people learn stuff. What else have you seen that might work out for people?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I think that all variations here come into two categories. One categories are ways to drive more scaled economics. I've made millions. My North Star is to now make it tens. I've made tens. My North star is to do hundreds. That's what drives people from, it's not entrepreneurship, it's investing; it's not investing, it's private equity, et cetera."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Whether we describe that as a bad quest or a good quest is a decision for your listener. The other arc is around giving. Eastern philosophies that have been around for thousands of years talk about this as the sort of end state of happiness."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I think that maybe to be provocative, I think that it's okay for you in act one and act two to not predicate yourself around the notion of giving to others because this is maybe the time on the planet where you need to take and you need to create."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"But boy, if you're going to work on an act three and you have 30 years, regardless of where you are economically, if you feel like you can take that off the table, if you can find ways to give, whatever that means to you, however that translates to you, is that content, is that volunteer, is that starting a company that is more mission based, that is not my role, but I think that if you are able to do that for 30 years and be giving, not only is that going to be more fulfilling than your act one and act two, but it's tremendous for society, very empowering."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And that's where I commend you because you're giving through your passion but also making a livelihood. And I think that that's a very powerful blend that is hard to achieve in act one and act two given constraints. And that's the liberation that act three provides."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"What do you think your act three plus ends up being?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Many have asked me about this. I would say that I'll use the word, and then I'll tell you I won't use that word. So it is around my passion around coaching and giving to others."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"But because I'm a product person, because I've seen success in building products, thinking about scale, thinking about community, I definitively plan to devote my act three towards coaching and giving to others and lifting up those that with the right advice at the right time can change their trajectory."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"But scaling that and doing that in a way that is very authentic is really the hardest part and it's product problem. So that's what I'll devote 30 years to and I look forward to that every day."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"That is beautiful. That feels like an exactly correct fit for you, and I'm here to help you on that journey any way I can."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Thank you, my friend."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Absolutely. Is there anything else you wanted to touch on before we get to our very exciting lightning round?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"No, I just appreciate your genuine offer to have me attend and participate in this wonderful podcast that you created."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"It's absolutely my pleasure. And it's not over yet. We've reached our very exciting lightning round. I've got six questions for you. Are you ready?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I am ready."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"What are two or three books that you've recommended most to other people?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"So two, and both business books. Sorry, I'm going to come across boring. But one is this sort of little bit of an old school book called Crossing the Chasm by Geoffrey Moore."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I don't know if other speakers have spoken about this, but it's a book that Geoffrey Moore wrote, and it's a book that really talks about how to get your first product on base. So it's this concept of creating a beachhead. I like that concept. Marketing is something that we don't talk about in enough and product."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And then the second one is a book that none of your listeners have actually probably heard of called Leadership and Self-Deception. It's a six-hour audio that I highly recommend."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"It's a story about a person who has hit a wall and who's getting all this feedback that they don't know what to make of, and it's around their mindset being stuck in a box. That was very powerful when I listened to it in my late 20s. So I encourage all of your listeners to grab that one."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"It's not one that anyone normally would hit, but it's a fun story. It's a good ride, and I think maybe you'll get something out of it."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I have not heard of that second one. I'm excited to check it out. I have Crossing the Chasm back behind me on that shelf somewhere."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And you talk about how marketing isn't something product leaders and managers think about enough, and I have many marketing-oriented guests on this podcast and those episodes do the least well, but I'm just going to keep doing it because I totally agree with you. I think there's so much to learn from marketing."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"It's connected to growth, which is connected to product. So I agree."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Yeah, and I think that marketing is a language of connecting products with people. That is what a product manager does, but we often lack the language. We lack the thinking around how to explain it, and yet we spend all our time on data and features for that."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The diversity of having both playbooks can make one of just a much more powerful builder. So I agree with you. Marketing folks is probably some of the best content and the least listen to. So maybe that's a plug for people to go back to those episodes."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"100%. That's what everyone should do. I don't know if you know this, but actually, at Airbnb, the product manager function has been renamed to product marketing. So all the product managers are product marketers because Brian is so big on, you're not just building product, your job is also to make sure people use it."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"We'll see how that experiment goes, but that's a bold move I thought."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Very much so. Very much. But it's an homage to this concept."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Exactly. Okay, back to landing ground. What is a favorite recent movie or TV show?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I'm a huge sports fan, so I have tickets to the Warriors and 49ers, and I am a big Bay Area sports fan. Giannis is my son's favorite player."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"He's a basketball player for the Milwaukee Bucks, and they have this Disney+ story called the Rise story, and it's a story about his childhood and how he struggled to find notoriety and how he made it into the professional leagues. It's a great Disney+ family show and it's a great kind of zero to hero type thing."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"So I love that story."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I feel like you're going to have a really good answer to this next one. What is a favorite interview question that you like to ask?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I like the format of, what's something that everyone takes for granted that you think is essentially hogwash or inaccurate? Sometimes I'll ask a manager, \"Look, you've managed hundreds of people in your career, what's conventional wisdom that you bet against that you have found is actually inaccurate?\""}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And you can do that for what do people think about AI, that's inaccurate, that everyone believes you could do that for domains, you can do all kinds of things."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I love it. Is there something you specifically look for there, or is it just depends on what you hear?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I'm always looking for people to break this sort of interview mindset. Everyone always prepares for interviews and then their entire conversation is predicting what you think you want me to say. And as a result, you can have high-quality people that you dismiss because they weren't genuine."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"There's no way to answer that question without being genuinely opinionated because it starts with, \"What is the thing that you think I want to say here? And then tell me why it's inaccurate.\" So when I break that wall, I'm testing, is this person authentic?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Because sometimes I'm dismissing them because they told me nothing new, but I don't want the interview process to penalize them. And this was my save question, but I can't use it now that I've told everyone."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"It's going to be all over TikTok soon. Everyone's going to know this. Next question, what's a favorite recent product that you've discovered that you love?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The geeky answer in me is the Arc Browser, which I think probably a lot of folks are starting to use and your listeners. Part of the reason is, I think it's just great for folks that have hundreds of tabs, and if you work at a scaled organization, you just have lots of tabs."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"But I think it's also, as a product guy, I thought Chrome was pretty good. They've got gajillions of people using it and billions of installs. So at some point, you kind of come to the conclusion that this is probably good enough."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And then you see a product obviously built with a much smaller team and you're like, \"Huh, there actually is opportunities to innovate.\" And any time you see a innovation on something that's mature, as a product person, I think that's just fascinating."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And I was just blown away at how they created something that's better than something I hold as a true set."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Yeah. We had Josh on the podcast, we talked about a lot of their philosophies. And on the tab thing, I think the key there is it closes your tabs after 24 hours unless you put them in a specific place, which I love because I was like, you think you would do that, but you don't."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And then it's broke so beautiful, you wake up in the morning, everything's gone, but you can save stuff that you want. The other thing I'll mention with Arc, by the way, also huge fan, that's all I use, and I'm not an investor, just a fan, is the onboarding experience is the best onboarding experience I've seen."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I was just like, I did it and I got a tweet about this. This is so good. And actually, if you go to that episode, there's a link to get past the wait list and just-"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Oh, that's right. Yeah."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Yeah. You gave me many thousands of invites. Okay, keep going. What is something relatively minor you've changed in the way you develop products and your team that's had a big impact on the team's ability to execute?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"A little bit of this is just because of scale, but oftentimes we think a lot about the products and the features and the decisions that we're working on, and then we think that meetings are a nuisance or a must-have necessary evil to be able to deliver."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Sometimes I realize that at a scaled organization, the meeting operating system is as important as the products that we're building because it sort of speaks to how we scale and how we ensure we have the right degree of delegation, the right conversations, and then the right acceleration on the right decisions."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"So what's interesting is every quarter, in my current teams, even in my past teams, I talk about our meetings like a product."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"We're on version seven in my team, and so we're like, \"Hey, version seven, every 90 days, these are the meetings, these are the discussions, this is how we organize, these are the attendees, and then here's how we make decisions. This is the cadence of the week."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"This is when people can work from home, hybrid, whatever it might be.\" And then I take feedback two months in and then every three months we make another route. What it finds is that people then can plan and they can make meeting time effective. And meeting time is such precious time."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"It's the most expensive time in a company. So when I was in a startup, I couldn't imagine doing this, but now this is like my bread and butter as a leader. It's the process part."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And frankly, for new folks that are new in leadership positions in a new company, it's the one thing you can do when you have low context. When you don't know how the product works, you can look at things with fresh eyes and see inefficiencies when everyone that's been in the system can't see it."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I'm a huge fan of rebooting meetings first. So process first, then people, then product, then strategy is sort of the notion I make, and this is this first thing I always do."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Final question, what is one thing every PM listening should do to help their career?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Ensure that the story you will tell about the work you're doing today is meaningful for your skip job. So if you sit down and you write down, \"In six months, in 12 months, in 24 months, when I achieve or finish this role, here's the paragraph I'll write. Here's a problem I solved. Here's the skill I built."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Here's the headwind I faced. Here's what I did to overcome it.\" Use I in the sentence, do not use we. We will do good things. You are who we are thinking about, your career. We're not looking for we. Master the story now. Understand the story."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"If the story sucks, you probably should be thinking through how to make the story not suck. But that to me is a very good career decision and I think everyone is building their story today. I want to know that story. I want that story to be incredibly compelling because whether you promote it or not, that story's compelling."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"You'll be promoted in career. And that's what we're here for."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl, this is the first time we've ever met. I'm such a fan instantly. This might be my new favorite episode. I'm so excited for people to listen to this. There's so much value here. Two final questions before we wrap up. Where can folks find you online if they want to learn more?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And also, talk about maybe various community, The Skip, and all that stuff that people can check out. And then how can listeners be useful to you?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I'm building this brand around The Skip because I'm so passionate. There's two outlets that people can easily connect. One is the podcast, which much like yourselves is available on Apple and Spotify and others. So I'd love for people to join my podcast and hear. What I'm now moving my podcast to is almost like coaching calls."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Because I have so many of them, I'm saying like, \"Hey, 30 minutes, let me walk you through a problem and hear how I'm thinking about it, whether that's a transition discussion or compensation discussion, et cetera.\""}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And then the other one is this sort of newsletter that I have on Substack, which is a bit of a mirror of the podcast. It's different forms of the same topic areas. So, would love for your listeners to connect with that."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I think as far as getting in touch with me, LinkedIn is where I spend most of my time professionally. So between Twitter and LinkedIn, my presence is relatively easy to find."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And then how listeners can help me, I mean, one, you can build the most fulfilling career story and be your best, but also give back and pull others forward. Whether that's through your act three or whether that's just helping others, I mean, I think that would be the most fulfilling to me."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"I think feedback from your listeners to me on things they wish I would spend time talking about is incredibly empowering for my content because then I can deliver more meaningful content. It's very different from yours, but I think it's all around the arc of trying to help people gain forward and be more effective tech professionals."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"So I would love to hear from your listeners."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Just to make sure people know where to go to do this. For feedback, do you recommend asdLinkedIn?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Yeah, LinkedIn is the ideal, but you can also find me on Twitter if you are just trying to add a quick... If you're trying to follow me, follow me on LinkedIn. If you're looking for feedback, just tweet me."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"And then for The Skip newsletter, what is the URL to go check that out?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"It's theskip.substack.com."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Amazing. And you don't publish often, but each issue is incredibly valuable, so we'll definitely link to that all in the show notes. Nikhyl, thank you again so much for being here. I will let you go now. This was amazing."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Yeah, thank you, Lenny. Appreciate it."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Lenny"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Bye, everyone. Thank you so much for listening. If you found this valuable, you can subscribe to the show on Apple Podcasts, Spotify, or your favorite podcast app. Also, please consider giving us a rating or leaving a review as that really helps other listeners find the podcast."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"You can find all past episodes or learn more about the show at lennyspodcast.com. See you in the next episode."}]}]}

syntheses.title: Synthesis on Modern Product Management Challenges and the Product Operating Model
syntheses.content: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Key Themes"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Marty Cagan's extensive discussion highlights several pivotal themes shaping contemporary product management. Foremost is the critique of \"product management theater,\" where companies overhire roles such as product owners, agile coaches, and product ops staff who predominantly perform project management rather than true product management. This phenomenon leads to inflated costs with limited outcome-driven value. Cagan contrasts feature teams, which focus on delivering predefined outputs (features), with empowered product teams that solve customer and business problems with a focus on outcomes and value. The distinction underscores the necessity of product managers who are creators responsible for value and viability, not mere backlog administrators."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Another key theme is transformation: moving organizations from feature team models to a product operating model characterized by 20 core principles and four essential competencies (product manager, product designer, tech lead, and product leader). Cagan emphasizes that such transformation is challenging but essential for innovation, velocity, and survival, especially amid macroeconomic pressures and the disruptive potential of generative AI. He also stresses the importance of critical thinking and continuous skill development for product managers to remain relevant."}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Patterns"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"A recurring pattern is the misalignment between the title of product manager and the actual role performed. Many so-called product managers operate as project managers within feature teams, leading to frustration and underutilization of potential product management impact. This misalignment tends to propagate through popular certification programs, online content, and community discussions, which mostly reflect suboptimal practices rather than the empowered product manager model."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"There is also a pattern where companies outside Silicon Valley, especially larger enterprises, have bloated teams with multiple assistant roles and heavy reliance on processes such as SAFe, which paradoxically reduce innovation and velocity. Conversely, high-performing companies operate with smaller, empowered teams that embrace experimentation, rapid iteration, and outcome measurement."}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tensions and Contradictions"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"A significant tension exists between the desire for predictability and process adherence versus the need for innovation and learning. Many organizations prioritize rigid frameworks and multiple roles to control delivery but sacrifice creativity and customer-centric outcomes. This creates a resistance to adopting empowered product teams despite evidence of their superior results."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Another contradiction arises in the role of product ops and similar assistant roles. While these roles can be valuable when focused on user research and data analytics as a small, high-leverage team, many companies misuse product ops as a process or governance function, or as an assistant to overwhelmed product managers, which undermines the discipline and adds unnecessary overhead."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"The timing of hiring product managers also involves tension. Founders often resist hiring PMs early, fearing it slows down decision-making. However, hiring too late risks chaotic scaling. Cagan advises that after product-market fit and a certain engineering scale, bringing in skilled PMs focused on value and viability is critical."}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Implications for Strategy and Decision-Making"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Organizations must critically evaluate their product management structures, especially in light of cost pressures and the disruptive potential of AI. The evidence suggests that reducing role bloat, focusing on empowered product teams, and emphasizing outcome-driven metrics will yield superior innovation and business results. Leaders should invest in developing the four key competencies and embrace transformation strategies such as pilot teams and spreading change incrementally."}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Product managers themselves need to take ownership of their career progression by developing skills in customer insight, data analysis, and business viability, moving beyond backlog administration. Critical thinking and skepticism toward popular but shallow product management content are essential. Additionally, companies should reconsider the role and scope of product ops, ensuring it supplements rather than substitutes product leadership."}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Open Questions"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"How quickly will generative AI disrupt traditional product management functions, and what new roles or competencies will emerge to address AI’s impact on viability and ethical considerations? How can companies outside Silicon Valley overcome entrenched process-heavy cultures and role bloat to adopt the product operating model effectively? What are the best practices for transforming legacy organizations while minimizing disruption?"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Finally, how can the product community better surface and amplify the voices and practices of high-performing companies to counteract the propagation of suboptimal product management advice?"}]}]}

syntheses.title: Navigating Product Management Careers: Long-Term Growth, Managerial Challenges, and Sustainable Leadership
syntheses.content: {"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Key Themes"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Nikhyl Singhal's extensive insights from his career and mentorship in product management reveal several key themes: the critical importance of long-term career planning beyond immediate promotions; the hazards of being in \"ex-growth\" companies without clear product-market fit; the evolving nature and challenges of managerial roles in tech; the rise and legitimacy of senior individual contributor (IC) paths; the difficulty senior leaders face with hidden developmental blind spots linked to their \"superpowers\"; and the mental health challenges that often accompany reaching senior leadership."}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Patterns"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"A recurring pattern is the tension between short-term gratification and long-term career health. Early-career PMs often focus on immediate promotions or escaping difficult bosses, which Singhal warns can lead to stagnation or dissatisfaction. Instead, he advocates for envisioning not just the next job but the \"job after next,\" building a career like a product roadmap. Another pattern is the mismatch between formal feedback systems and the real, sometimes contradictory, feedback needed to identify development areas, especially for senior leaders whose strengths may mask critical weaknesses. The emergence of senior IC tracks parallels engineering and design, reflecting a broader industry correction to premature pushes into management."}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Tensions"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Several tensions arise in the analysis: the balance between acquiring prestigious company \"logos\" on a resume versus pursuing meaningful, diverse building experiences; the loyalty to a company in challenging product-market fit situations versus the personal financial and career risks of remaining in ex-growth companies; managing versus individual contributor roles, where managerial power does not guarantee managerial skill or satisfaction; and the discomfort senior leaders feel when their defining strengths become impediments to further growth."}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Implications for Strategy and Decision-Making"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"For individuals and organizations, Singhal's insights suggest several strategic considerations: 1) Career planning should be framed with a multi-step horizon, encouraging professionals to build skills and narratives that support longer-term mobility rather than chasing immediate promotions or escapes. 2) Talent should be wary of joining or remaining in companies lacking clear product-market fit despite high valuations, as these \"ex-growth\" companies pose significant career and financial risks. 3) Companies need to invest more deliberately in managerial training and feedback mechanisms, recognizing that managerial skill is distinct from technical or product skills. 4) Establishing and legitimizing senior IC career paths is vital to retain and develop top talent who prefer to build rather than manage. 5) Leadership development programs should incorporate tools to uncover and address the \"shadows of superpowers\"—the blind spots that senior leaders develop as a byproduct of their core strengths. 6) Both individuals and organizations must prioritize mental health and career fulfillment beyond traditional success metrics, especially considering the extended career arcs now common in tech."}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Open Questions"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Open questions remain around how best to scale authentic, trust-based communities for product leaders to share challenges and feedback without sacrificing intimacy. Additionally, as the industry continues to evolve, how will the senior IC path mature and be recognized equivalently to management tracks? What frameworks can organizations develop to better identify and remediate leadership blind spots before they become career-limiting? Lastly, how can mental health and long-term career motivation be integrated into leadership development at scale to prevent the existential crises that Singhal describes among senior leaders?"}]}]}

artifacts.title: Overview of Contemporary Challenges and Growth in Product Management
artifacts.content:
{"type":"doc","content":[{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Recommendation"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Organizations should transform their product management practices by adopting empowered product teams focused on outcomes, streamlining roles to reduce overhead, and investing in long-term career development pathways that balance managerial and individual contributor tracks."}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Confidence Level"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"High. The synthesis draws from recognized thought leaders Marty Cagan and Nikhyl Singhal, supported by observable industry patterns and challenges documented across multiple organizations."}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Problem Statement"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"Many organizations struggle with inefficient product management structures that emphasize process over outcomes, role bloat, and short-term career planning that limit innovation, velocity, and sustained leadership growth."}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Key Evidence"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"• Product management theater inflates costs without delivering outcome-driven value, with many so-called PMs acting as backlog administrators rather than empowered creators (Source: Marty Cagan).
• High-performing companies use small empowered teams with clear outcome focus, avoiding heavy processes like SAFe and multiple assistant roles (Source: Marty Cagan).
• Career growth in product management requires long-term planning beyond immediate promotions, incorporating diverse experiences and recognizing senior individual contributor paths (Source: Nikhyl Singhal).
• Managerial skill development is distinct from product expertise and needs deliberate organizational investment, including mechanisms to uncover leadership blind spots (Source: Nikhyl Singhal).
• Mental health and sustainable leadership are critical considerations given the pressures on senior product leaders (Source: Nikhyl Singhal).
• There is tension in balancing predictability and innovation in product teams, and between managerial and IC career tracks (Sources: Both).
• The disruptive potential of generative AI introduces new uncertainties and opportunities in product management roles and competencies (Source: Marty Cagan)."}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Recommended Direction"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"1. Conduct a critical assessment of current product management roles and processes to identify and eliminate non-value-adding roles and activities.
2. Shift to empowered product teams that own customer and business problem solving with clear outcome metrics.
3. Invest in developing the four core product competencies: product management, product design, technology leadership, and product leadership.
4. Build long-term career frameworks supporting both managerial and senior individual contributor tracks, with robust feedback and leadership development programs.
5. Incorporate mental health and sustainable leadership principles into organizational culture and leadership training.
6. Pilot incremental transformation initiatives to reduce disruption and demonstrate value.
7. Monitor and prepare for the impact of generative AI on product management functions, updating skill requirements accordingly."}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Open Questions"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"• How rapidly will generative AI reshape product management roles and what new competencies will emerge?
• What strategies best enable legacy and non-Silicon Valley enterprises to adopt empowered product operating models?
• How can organizations scale authentic, trust-based communities for leadership feedback and mental health support?
• What frameworks can effectively identify and remediate senior leadership blind spots before impacting careers?
• How will senior individual contributor career paths evolve and gain parity with management tracks?"}]},{"type":"heading","attrs":{"level":2},"content":[{"type":"text","text":"Dissenting Signals"}]},{"type":"paragraph","content":[{"type":"text","text":"• Resistance to reducing process and role layers may arise from organizational culture valuing predictability and control over innovation.
• The transition to empowered product teams can be disruptive and resource-intensive, potentially impacting short-term delivery.
• Some organizations may find it challenging to balance managerial and IC career tracks without clear role definitions and incentives.
• The pace of AI disruption is uncertain, and premature investment in AI-related competencies may divert focus from current challenges.
• Mental health initiatives require sustained commitment and may be difficult to embed in traditionally high-pressure tech environments."}]}]}
